questions.question: State two key differences between IoT and M2M concerning data usage. (4 marks)
keyPoints: M2M data is typically used for an immediate, specific task or trigger with limited analysis.
IoT data is aggregated in the cloud and used for big data analytics, pattern recognition, and intelligent decision-making.

questions.question: Explain the difference between IoT and M2M with the help of a suitable example for each. Discuss how technologies like SDN and NFV facilitate IoT scalability. (10 marks)
keyPoints: Definition of M2M with example (e.g., a standalone smart meter sending usage data).
Definition of IoT with example (e.g., a smart home system integrating lights, thermostat, and security).
Contrast points: Internet vs. non-IP networks, data integration vs. siloed data, intelligence vs. simple commands.
Explanation of SDN: Centralized control of network infrastructure for flexible management of many devices.
Explanation of NFV: Replacing dedicated hardware with software on standard servers for cost-effective scaling.
Conclusion on how these technologies enable the vast, scalable networks required for IoT.

questions.question: Differentiate between M2M and IoT based on communication protocols and interoperability. (4 marks)
keyPoints: M2M often uses non-IP protocols (Zigbee, Z-Wave, proprietary standards) in closed, vendor-specific ecosystems.
IoT primarily uses IP-based protocols (HTTP, MQTT, CoAP) enabling open, interoperable, internet-connected ecosystems.
IoT systems are designed for horizontal integration across verticals; M2M systems are typically vertical silos.

questions.question: Analyze a smart factory scenario and identify which aspects represent M2M and which represent IoT. Explain the evolution from M2M to IoT in this context. (10 marks)
keyPoints: M2M aspect: Individual vibration sensors on motors sending alerts to a local controller (point-to-point, single purpose).
IoT aspect: Sensors feed data to a cloud platform that correlates vibration data with production schedules, temperature, and supply chain data.
IoT value: Predictive maintenance, automated spare parts ordering, production optimization through data integration.
Evolution: The factory moved from isolated monitoring (M2M) to connected intelligence (IoT) by adding cloud, analytics, and cross-system integration.
Enabling technologies: SDN/NFV provide the scalable network infrastructure; NETCONF/YANG enable standardized device management.
